coredata.dc:description: The carbon dots (CDs) have aroused tremendous attention as corrosion inhibitors. However, the formation process of CDs and the rational regulation of the microstructure of CDs are great challenges to study. Herein, a controlled solvothermal synthesis for CDs (CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4) from L-cysteine and different ionic liquid precursors has been investigated for regulating the microstructure of CDs. In the preparation of CDs, ionic liquids (ILs) act as modifiers, carbon sources, and reaction media. The charged nature of IL effectively suppresses CDs agglomeration and enhances the CD stability. The influence of sulfonic-acid functionalization on the CDs microstructure is found to promote the formation of pyrrolic N species. The results demonstrate that -SO3H functionalized ionic liquids enable the derived CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-SO3Cl to exhibit the superior inhibition performance. Specifically, inhibition efficiency of CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-SO3Cl for 45# steel in 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution reached 92.63 % and 92.78 % at 50 mg·L−1, respectively. The adsorption film formed on the steel surface prevents contact with corrosive mediums through both physical and chemical adsorption of CDs. The work provided a strategy for the rational design of CDs as efficient corrosion inhibitors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon dots (CDs) have aroused tremendous attention as corrosion inhibitors. However, the formation process of CDs and the rational regulation of the microstructure of CDs are great challenges to study. Herein, a controlled solvothermal synthesis for CDs (CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4) from L-cysteine and different ionic liquid precursors has been investigated for regulating the microstructure of CDs. In the preparation of CDs, ionic liquids (ILs) act as modifiers, carbon sources, and reaction media. The charged nature of IL effectively suppresses CDs agglomeration and enhances the CD stability. The influence of sulfonic-acid functionalization on the CDs microstructure is found to promote the formation of pyrrolic N species. The results demonstrate that -SO3H functionalized ionic liquids enable the derived CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-SO3Cl to exhibit the superior inhibition performance. Specifically, inhibition efficiency of CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-SO3Cl for 45# steel in 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution reached 92.63 % and 92.78 % at 50 mg·L−1, respectively. The adsorption film formed on the steel surface prevents contact with corrosive mediums through both physical and chemical adsorption of CDs. The work provided a strategy for the rational design of CDs as efficient corrosion inhibitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a type of zero-dimensional nanomaterial, are extensively used in biological imaging, light-emitting diodes, metal detection, anti-microbial inhibitor, photocatalysts, monitoring of matter, and information encryption due to their excellent photophysical and photochemical properties [1–5]. Generally, the bottom-up production method of CDs from small organic molecules involves polycondensation, cross-linking, and carbonization process [6]. However, the CDs were synthesized through the semi-empirical method, through modulation resource, solvent, and experimental parameters, including reaction time and temperature [7]. The lack of theoretical guidance and strategic approaches impedes the precise engineering of design and functionalized CDs at the atomic and molecular scales. Therefore, it is particularly important to precisely manipulate CD characteristics and surface state, including surface functional groups and element dope.

One interesting method is the incorporation of additives during the CD preparation to affect the nature of CDs. Ionic liquids, usually composed of organic cations and a small amount of organic or inorganic anions, have excellent physical and chemical properties, such as excellent chemical and thermal stability, vapor pressure, and designability of cation and anion parts [6–8]. The properties of the ionic liquid can be controlled by changing the structure of the cation and anion. The synthesis of CDs using ionic liquids (ILs) is a new innovative method [9–11]. The ionic liquids participate in the production of CDs as modifiers, solvents, and carbon precursors [12–14], providing more advantages to their introduction of ILs into the preparation of CDs [12]. In detail, the low interfacial tension of the ILs is beneficial to the rapid nucleation of CDs. Additionally, their low surface energy promotes CD stability, which is favorable for inhibiting the accumulation of CDs. The use of ILs as precursors for the synthesis of heteroatom-doped CDs can be attributed to their heteroatom-enriched structures [12,15]. In our previous work, a series of N, S-doped CDs were synthesized by the solvothermal method, in which L-cysteine as the carbon source and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([BMIm]Br) as the solvent [16]. We found that the N-type content of CDs was adjusted by reaction conditions [16]. Howethever, there is a lack of in-depth understanding of the relationship between ILs and CDs structures. In addition, a few investigations reported using Brönsted acidic ionic liquids to assist with controlling CDs properties.

Recently, CDs have been applied in the anticorrosion field due to their super inhibition properties [17,18]. The CD inhibitors, characterized by their numerous conjugated systems and functional groups, display outstanding adsorptive properties on metal surfaces [17,19,20]. The doping of heteroatoms, including N and S elements, has emerged as one of the most effective strategies for enhancing the inhibition performance of CDs [21]. The CDs have development potential in acid environments as inhibitors. Thus, in the present study, we further investigated the inhibition performance of CDs in which -SO3H-functionalized ionic liquids were used during the preparation. The study seeks to answer the following two questions: How do the ionic liquids affect the properties of the CDs? How do these ILs, -SO3H-functionalized imidazolium cations, and anions affect the CD corrosion inhibition performance?

In this work, we describe a synthetic route for CDs using an ionic liquid-assisted solvothermal method. Herein, the L-cysteine as a carbon source and three ionic liquids, 1-propyl sulfonate-3-methylimidazolium hydrogen sulfate ([PSO3HMIm][HSO4]), 1-(3-propyl sulfonic acid)-3-methylimidazole chloride ([PSO3HMIm]Cl) and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hydrogen sulfate ([BMIm][HSO4]), were used to prepare N, S-doped CDs, respectively. The morphology, construction, and properties of CDs can be modulated by the IL structures. The Brönsted acid ionic liquids, as catalysts, promotes reaction activities of the polycondensation and crosslinking process during the formation of CDs. Furthermore, these CDs were used as corrosion inhibitors to protect carbon steel (45# steel) in 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution, and the inhibition performance of the CDs inhibitor was assessed. The impact of ionic liquids on the corrosion inhibition efficiency of CDs was investigated. Finally, the corrosion inhibition mechanism of the CDs inhibitor was proposed.

The 1-Methylimidazole (AR, 99 %) and 1,3-Propanesultone (AR, 99 %) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The L-cysteine (L-cys, AR, 99 %) was obtained from Shanghai Lanji, and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, AR, 98 %) was acquired from Tianjin Fengchuang Chemical Reagent Science and Technology Co., Ltd. All reagents were used directly without purification.

The preparation routes of ILs, including 1-propylsulfonic-3-methylimidazolium hydrogen ([PSO3HMIm][HSO4]), 1-propylsulfonic-3-methylimidazolium chloride ([PSO3HMIm]Cl), and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hydrogen sulfate ([BMIm][HSO4]) are shown in Fig. S1, Fig. S2 and Fig. S3. The [PSO3HMIm][HSO4] was synthesized in our laboratory using a similar procedure according to the references [22]. The [BMIm][HSO4] and [PSO3HMIm]Cl were synthesized according to the references [23,24].

The preparation of CDs is shown in Fig. 1. Initially, 5 g of L-cysteine and 2 g of [PSO3HMIm][HSO4] were combined in a beaker and stirred using a glass rod until well mixed. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a digestion tank and subjected to reaction temperatures of 220 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was dispersed into 60 mL of deionized water and subjected to 20 min of ultrasonic treatment. The product was centrifuged for 15 min at 8000 r·min−1 to collect the supernatant. A 1000 Da dialysis bag was utilized to treat the supernatant for 24 h, and deionized water was changed every 6 h. The resulting yellow solution was placed in a vacuum-drying oven to obtain the product CDs-SO3HSO4. If the [PSO3HMIm][HSO4] was replaced by [PSO3HMIm]Cl or [BMIm][HSO4], CDs-SO3Cl and CDs-BuHSO4 were prepared under the same conditions, respectively.

The UV and PL spectrum of each CDs (dispersed in deionized water) was performed with UV-Vis using Thermo Scientific, Evolution 300, and PL (F-380). The FT-IR spectra of CDs were recorded on Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS50. Thermogravimetric (TG) curves of L-cys and all CDs were measured by NETZSCH STA 2500 Regulus instrument. The 5 mg of CDs and L-cys were weighed. Under the N2 atmosphere, the temperature was increased from room temperature to 100°C and maintained for 30 min. Subsequently, the temperature was increased to 700°C at a rate of 5 °C·min−1. The 13C NMR spectra of all CDs were collected by Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (Bruker, AVANCE, AV 400 MHz). The XPS provided a comprehensive analysis of the elemental composition of CDs using Thermo Fisher, NEXSA. The morphology was obtained from a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Japan, JEM-F200).

The Gamry (Reference 3000) electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system was used for recording the signal of electrochemical measurements. The counter electrode was made of platinum sheet, and the reference electrode consisted of Ag/AgCl and lugging capillary. The 45# steel, covered with epoxy resin with a controlled exposed surface of 1 cm2, served as the working electrode. The test electrolyte used in the test comprised a 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution with varying concentrations of CDs, and the total volume of the electrolyte was 200 mL. The preparation of the test electrolyte is described in the supplementary material. Before each test, the working electrode was sanded with abrasive paper with grit sizes of #180, #600, #1000, #1500, and #2000, respectively. The electrode was thoroughly cleaned with deionized water and isopropyl alcohol before drying. The 45# steel was immersed in the test solution for 1 h until a steady state was achieved, acquiring enduring values of open circuit potential (E ocp). For the EIS, the initial potential was E ocp. The test frequency range was 100 kHz∼50 mHz, and the amplitude was 10 mV. The polarization test was conducted within the potential range of ± 250 mV versus E ocp, and the scan rate of 0.5 mV·s−1.

Before the surface characterization, the 45# steel specimens were polished and cleaned. The 45# steel specimens were immersed in 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution without/with CDs of 200 mg·L−1 and the differences of morphology were analyzed by SEM (Phenom Prox) before and after CD corrosion inhibition. The chemical composition of corrosion products was analyzed using XPS.

The FT-IR spectra of the three CD inhibitors are presented in Fig. 2a. There are prominent absorption peaks at 3443, 3140, 1663, 1400 and 1044 cm−1, which correspond to N-H, O-H, CO, C-N and C-O bonds, respectively [25–28]. The peaks observed at 1574 cm−1 are attributed to the amide bond (-CONH-), indicating that the CDs were obtained by dehydration condensation of L-cysteine [29]. In addition, the peak at 614 cm−1 indicates the existence C-S bond [30,31]. Significantly, the symmetrical stretching vibration of SO bonds are located at 1180 and 1125 cm−1 [32].

For the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 2b), two distinct absorption bands were observed for as-prepared CDs and were attributed to the CC bond at 200–219 nm and n-π* transitions of the carbon core at 300–400 nm [33]. Moreover, the as-obtained CDs water solutions appear yellow in daylight and a clear and bright blue under a 365 nm UV light (Fig. S5). Fig. S6a, S6b, and S6c display the PL emission spectra of CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4, respectively. The maximum excitation wavelengths of CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4 were 380, 370, and 350 nm, with the corresponding emission wavelengths of 449, 438, and 426 nm, respectively. The CDs exhibited different emission wavelengths, indicating the discrepancy in the surface functional group of the as-prepared CDs. The substitutional heteroatoms and functional groups on the surfaces of CDs cause a fluorescence redshift. The change in the surface state of CDs is ascribed to the IL precursor structures.

The 13C NMR spectra of three CDs provide more structure information, as shown in Fig. S7-S9. The chemical shifts observed in the range of 0–70 ppm indicate the presence of sp3 hybridization of carbon atoms, which originate from the aliphatic carbon present on the surface of CDs. Moreover, the sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms, which are part of the conjugated structures within the CDs, contribute to the resonances in the region of 110–140 ppm. The new chemical shift at 150–180 ppm was observed in the spectra of all three CDs, suggesting that the precursor experienced dehydration and polymerization reactions during the solvothermal treatment. The difference in IL structures resulted in the microstructure discrepancies of the CDs. Obviously, the NMR spectra of CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-SO3Cl show only chemical shifts at 156 ppm (Fig. 2c), which reveals only one chemical environment for the amide group. In contrast, CDs-BuHSO4 exhibits multiple chemical shifts at 150–180 ppm.

The thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was used to conduct a in-depth analysis of the thermal stability of the CDs. The TG result (Fig. 2d) displays obvious mass loss and gives evidence that functional groups exist on the CD surface [34,35]. A sharp mass decrease was observed for L-cys. Differently, the CDs display a particular thermal degradation process. The thermal degradation at 200–300 °C is ascribed to the thermal decomposition of functional groups, such as amino groups and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs. The thermal degradation at 300–400 °C is attributed to the pyrolysis of polymer chain segments. The carbon cores of all the three CDs show the weight loss after 400°C, among which the CDs-SO3HSO4 has a minimal weight loss, suggesting the highest conjugated structure in the carbon cores, facilitating the transfer of electrons.

The presence of four elements, including C, N, O, and S, was confirmed ( Fig. 3e), which proves the CDs constructed are all N and S-doped. In the C 1s spectra shown in Fig. 3a, three peaks can be discerned, namely C-C or CC bond at 284.8 eV, C-N/C-S bond at 285.8 eV, and CO bond at 288.2 eV [30]. The existence of C-N/C-S proved that the N and S elements were successfully incorporated into CD inhibitors. Three types of N species, with 399.2 eV for pyridine N, 400.4 ± 0.1 eV for pyrrolic N, and 401.2 eV for graphitic N, were observed in the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 3b) [21]. Recent research shows that the CD inhibitors can be chemically adsorbed on the carbon steel surface in which pyrrolic nitrogen can share electrons with the 3d orbital of the iron atoms [17,21,36]. The corresponding N-types of CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4 are displayed in Fig. 3f. The percentages of pyrrolic nitrogen in CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4 were 69.56 %, 71.49 %, and 51.81 %, respectively. The percentages of graphitic nitrogen in CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4 were found to be 14.79 %, 17.35 %, and 20.84 %, respectively. SO3H-functionalized ILs effectively promote the formation of pyrrolic nitrogen. In the O 1 s spectra (Fig. 3c), three peaks are observed at 530.9, 531.8, and 532.9 eV, corresponding to the CO, O-H, and C-O bond, respectively [37–40]. In the S 2p spectra (Fig. 3d), six characteristic peaks are displayed, which are respectively assigned to the C-S bond at S 2p 3/2 (163.3 ± 0.1 eV), S 2p 1/2 (164.5 ± 0.1 eV), C-SOx groups at the S 2p 3/2 (167.7 ± 0.1 eV) and S 2p 1/2 (168.8 ± 0.1 eV) and S2- groups at the S 2p 3/2 of 161.7 eV, S 2p 1/2 of 162.8 eV [41–44].

The TEM images ( Fig. 4) show that the as-prepared CDs were uniformly spherical nanoparticles. The average diameters of CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4 were 5.8, 5.0, and 10 nm, respectively. The utilization of SO3H-functionalized IL offers the advantage of achieving CD with diminished dimensions. The influence of the amount of -SO3H functionalized ionic liquids on the CD structure was investigated. An increased amount (3 g) of [PSO3HMIm][HSO4] was used for the synthesis of CDs, named CDs-1, in which the dosage of L-cys and reaction conditions remained unchanged. The SEM (Fig. S12a.) of CD1 shows a uniform sphere with an average size of 4.15 nm, which is smaller than CDs-SO3HSO4. Notably, for CDs-1, only a single peak was observed in the range of 150–160 ppm at the 13C NMR spectra (Fig. S10), and a similar result can be obtained if mixed ionic liquids of 1 g [PSO3HMIm][HSO4] and 1 g [BMIm][HSO4] were used to synthesize CDs (named CDs-2), which further confirms the results that the existence of -SO3H leads to only one form of CO group (Fig. S11). The SEM (Fig. S12c.) of CDs-2 is uniform spherical with an increased size of 9.38 nm, which is in between the CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-BuHSO4. The difference in the size of CDs can be attributed to the role of acidic ionic liquids, which accelerated the amidation reaction and cross-linking reactions, thus promoting the rapid formation of the carbon core.

The open-circuit potential curves for 45# steel in the 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution without and with CDs are depicted in Fig. S13. With the immersion time prolonged, the E ocp of CDs tends to stabilize, indicating the working electrode is in steady condition, ready for further EIS test.

Figs. 5a, 5c, and 5e show the Nyquist diagrams of the investigated CD inhibitors in 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solutions. It is noted that Nyquist diagrams consist of capacitive reactance arcs and inductive reactance arcs. Generally, the capacitive reactance is ascribed to the charge transfer process occurring at the interface between the 45# steel and the H2SO4 solution. The deviation between the concave and the ideal capacitive semicircle is attributed to the irregular 45# steel surface. The small inductive loop is indicative of the adsorption and desorption process of the species (SO4 2-)ads and (H+)ads [45]. With the introduction of the CDs, the Nyquist plots show a similar semicircle shape as the blank solution, which means that the CDs do not change the corrosion mechanism of 45# steel in the H2SO4 solution. The concave semicircle diameter increases significantly with the addition of CDs, suggesting the enhanced inhibition performance of investigated CD inhibitors. In addition, the capacitance semicircle diameter gradually increases with the increase of the CD concentration.

From the Bode plot (Figs. 5b, 5d, and 5f), the Bode phase angle exists as a one-time constant. As the concentration of CDs increases, the frequency range exhibiting the maximum phase angle becomes bigger than that in the blank solution, manifesting the CDs on the 45# steel surface as the barrier layer for corrosion inhibition. The 45# steel in the 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution containing CD inhibitors displays a greater impedance modulus (lowest frequency), higher than the blank solution, showing that the corrosion of 45# steel is inhibited. In general, the higher the impedance modulus at the lowest frequency (|Z|lowest), the greater the corrosion inhibition of the sample. The |Z|lowest values increased proportionally with the concentration of CDs, indicating that the inhibitor performance of CDs is positively correlated with their concentration. The CDs-SO3HSO4 acquires the highest |Z|lowest value, revealing the best inhibitor performance among CDs-SO3Cl and CDs-BuHSO4.

From Table 1, the C

dl can be ascribed to dielectric constant reduction due to the CD inhibitors being substituted for water molecules at the interface between electrodes and solution to form the adsorption layer [46,47].

values were 97.00 % for 200 mg·L−1 of CDs-SO3HSO4. The improvement of the corrosion inhibition efficiency was lower after adding CD inhibitors because of partial desorption or saturation adsorption on the 45# steel surface. The CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-SO3Cl exhibited comparable values of inhibition efficiency of 97.00 % and 96.85 %, respectively. There is a small difference between the inhibition of CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-BuHSO4 (0.15 %). However, the difference in inhibition performance between CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4 tended to be more pronounced at a lower concentration. At 40 mg·L−1, the inhibition efficiencies of 87.98 % and 90.28 % were for CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-SO3Cl, respectively, much higher than CDs-BuHSO4 (only 32.07 %). At 50 mg·L−1, the inhibition efficiencies for CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4 were 92.35 %, 91.33 % and 56.19 %, respectively. At lower concentrations, CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-SO3Cl exhibited a much higher inhibition efficiency than CDs-BuHSO4.

To investigate the long-term inhibition efficiency, the EIS tests for different immersion times were carried out. Fig. S14 shows the Nyquist and Bode plots of 45# carbon steel and 200 mg·L−1 of CDs-SO3HSO4 after immersion in 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution with different immersion times. The diameter of the capacitive loop for CDs-SO3HSO4 is consistently higher than the blank solution during the immersion period. Significantly, after immersion in 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution by 200 mg·L−1 of CDs-SO3HSO4 for 24 h, the R p value of 387.6 Ω·cm2 is higher than the blank solution (Table S1), with the inhibition efficiency of 97.09 % at 24 h. Therefore, the CDs-SO3HSO4 exhibits long-term corrosion inhibition in 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution.

Fig. 7. displays the polarization (PDP) curves for the 45# steel in the presence and absence of CDs in a 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution. Table 2 presents the electrochemical parameters obtained from PDP curves. The polarization curve exhibits distinct shifts towards lower current densities after the addition of the investigated CD inhibitors, suggesting that both dissolution of anodic Fe and evolution of hydrogen depolarization were suppressed. The corrosion inhibition efficiency (η tafel) values were determined through the following equation:
(3)
η
Tafel
=
i

0
-
i

× 100 %
where corrosion current density without and with CDs is represented symbolically by
i
c o r r
0
and i corr. Table 2 shows the obvious decrease in i corr values when the H2SO4 solution contains CDs inhibitor, while corrosion inhibition performance increased with the CDs concentration. In addition, compared with the blank solution, the E corr deviated and moved to the negative potential direction, and the shift range was less than ± 85 mV after the addition of the studied CD inhibitors. The results indicated that the three CDs belonged to mixed type inhibitor, which means that both anodic and cathodic reactions were inhibited [48]. The variations observed in β a and β c values demonstrate that the addition of CDs influences the dynamic process of both anodic and cathodic reactions. This effect can be attributed to CDs molecules adsorbed on the steel surface, which can prevent the iron from contacting the corrosive medium (such as H3O+). The anodic reaction was mainly inhibited, which can be attributed to CDs occupying the anodic reaction site dominantly and improving the activation energy barrier of the reaction, as evidenced by the β a values being much more obvious than β c values [28,49]. With the increase of the polarization potential, the current is consistent with a blank solution, defined as anodic desorption, which can be attributed to substrate iron dissolution causing the desorption of CDs [50]. The η tafel of CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-SO3Cl were 97.00 % and 96.99 % and higher than that of CDs-BuHSO4 (95.56 %) at 200 mg·L−1. It is advantageous to get enhanced inhibition efficiency with CDs prepared through the utilization of functional ionic liquids. The role of cation species is crucial in determining inhibition performance. The CDs-SO3HSO4 showed an insignificant difference from CDs-SO3Cl in inhibition properties, suggesting that anions do not significantly contribute to the inhibitory effect. The η tafel of CDs-SO3HSO4 and CDs-SO3Cl are significantly higher than that of CDs-BuHSO4, with η tafel values of 87.24 %, 90.19 %, and 67.88 %, respectively, at the concentration of 40 mg·L−1. The polarization curves measurements are in agreement with the EIS results.

The Langmuir adsorption isotherm was obtained by Eq. (4).

The potentiodynamic polarization curves date was used to fit θ as a function of C inh. Fig. 8 displays the C inh/θ dependence straight-line on C inh, and the R 2 values are 0.9998, 0.9999, and 0.9968, respectively, indicating that the adsorption of CDs inhibition follows the Langmuir model. The

According to fitted data, the K ads values are 231.0, 229.0, and 59.92 L·g−1, respectively, exhibiting a decrease in the order: CDs-SO3HSO4＞CDs-SO3Cl＞CDs-BuHSO4. The

Generally, the higher K ads values indicate stronger adsorption between CD inhibitors and steel surfaces. The K ads and
Δ

values confirmed that CDs-SO3HSO4 had the best inhibition efficiency.

The SEM images of 45# steel specimens after 2 h immersion in 0.5 mol·L−1 H2SO4 solution without/ with CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl, and CDs-BuHSO4 of 200 mg·L−1 are depicted in Fig. 9. A rough surface with severe damage is observed (Fig. 9a), while Figs. 9b, 9c, and 9d illustrate a smooth surface, which could contribute to enhanced CDs adsorption on the 45# steel surface to reduce corrosion.

The XPS measurements were carried out to understand the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDs. The high-resolution spectra from the 45# steel surface adsorbed with CD inhibitors are displayed in
Figs. 10, 11. The N and S elemental peaks were observed on the 45# steel surface, suggesting the formation of the CD adsorption film. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 10a) can be fitted with three peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.9 eV, and 288.1 eV, corresponding to CC/C-C and C-N/C-S and CO, respectively [52], which proved the carbon dots were successfully covered on the metal surface. The N-Fe interaction was observed at 399.2 eV (Fig. 10b) [53,54], suggesting that lone pair electrons of sp2-hybridized nitrogen can establish coordination bonds with Fe and cover the 45# steel surface through chemical adsorption. The peak of protonated nitrogen species (N+H) located at 401.2 eV (Fig. 10b) [55] and peaks at 400.2 eV correspond to C-N bonds [56], indicating that adsorption layers of CD inhibitors formed on 45# steel substrate through electrostatic interaction. The 531.0, 531.7, and 532.5 eV peaks exhibited in the spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 10c) corresponded to O2- from iron oxide, adsorption of SO4 2-, and O-CO bond, respectively [57]. Besides, a distinct absorption belonging to -OH, which comes from the hydroxide of iron, is observed at 531 eV for the steel surface treated with CDs-SO3HSO4 [57]. From the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 10d), four deconvoluted peaks are observed for the S element. The peak at 163.3 and 164.5 eV is assigned to the S 2p 3/2 and S 2p 1/2 level of C-S bond, which manifested that the CD inhibitor was successfully adsorbed onto the surface of 45# steel [37]. The peak at 168.49 and 169.65 eV was ascribed to adsorbed SO4 2- [57,58]. In addition, the double signals at 161.8 and 163 eV are labeled to be sulfide (S2-) when typical ionic liquid [BMIm][HSO4] was used to prepare CDs [55–57]. The XPS results manifest that CD inhibitors can form CD adsorption films on the 45# steel surface because of the existence of significant interactions involving the inhibitor and the surface of 45# steel.

The corrosion inhibition mechanism of the CDs in the H2SO4 solution was clarified based on the above experimental data and previous publications [17]. The existence of physical and chemical adsorption between CDs and 45# steel surface is necessary to limit corrosion reaction, which maintains higher inhibition efficiency. The XPS results confirmed that the CD inhibitors can be adsorbed on steel surfaces and form a protective film. The steel surface immersion in H2SO4 solution becomes positively charged, and negatively charged SO4 2- particles collect at the steel surface to form the Fe[(H2O)nSO4 2-]ads. The CD inhibitors easily take on a positive charge due to the presence of N+H in CD inhibitors, ensuing electrostatic interaction to form the protective film on the steel surface [59]. The TG (Fig. 2d) confirmed that the CDs surface contained functional groups, which resulted in significant mass losses from 200°C to 300°C [34]. The CD inhibitor can compete for adsorptive sites with H3O+, preventing or slowing a corrosion reaction. The coordinate bonds between CDs and Fe surfaces were formed; the CDs contain various N species that can donate lone pair electrons to unoccupied 3d orbitals of Fe atoms [59–61]. The functional groups with N and S elements on CD surfaces provide the sites for adsorption. The pyridine ring almost perpendicularly adsorbs on the steel surface, while the pyrrole ring adsorbs with a parallel orientation, where π-complex bonds could form through the pyrrole ring susceptible to electrophilic attack. The parallel orientation of pyrrole-like N species reduced the exposure area for 45# steel in H2SO4 solution, which is favorable to reducing erosive attack [62]. Besides, the pyrrole-like N augments the interaction between CDs and the steel surface due to its heightened propensity for electron donation, which is beneficial to sharing electrons between the Fe atom and the CD inhibitor to form an adsorption film [36].

Novel N, S-doped CDs were synthesized by the solvothermal method from L-cysteine and IL precursors, including [PSO3HMIm][HSO4], [PSO3HMIm]Cl or [BMIm][HSO4]. The -SO3H functionalized imidazolium-based ionic liquids with strong acidity are beneficial to obtain small particle size CDs by accelerating dehydration and crosslinking of the precursor, and the S doping can be achieved as well. In addition, the -SO3H functionalized imidazolium-based ionic liquids promoted the formation of pyrrolic nitrogen species, enabling the resultant CDs to exhibit the superior inhibition performance, especially at lower concentrations. As a result, the inhibition efficiency of CDs-SO3HSO4, CDs-SO3Cl reaches 92.63 % and 92.78 %, at the concentrations of 50 mg·L−1, respectively. The CDs can be adsorbed on the 45# steel as a protective adsorption layer with abundant functional groups as adsorption sites, which obeys the Langmuir adsorption. The work provided a strategy for the rational regulation of microstructures of CD inhibitors with ionic liquids as the precursors.
